title: Contents Restoration & Pack-Out — Inventory, Cleaning, Storage & Insurance Documentation in Australia
description: A complete guide to contents restoration and pack-out operations for Australian water and fire damage jobs — systematic inventory, cleaning protocols, storage management, and insurance documentation. Covers scope writing and valuation for contents claims.
author: Phill McGurk | RestoreAssist
transcript:
## What Is Contents Restoration?

Contents restoration is the assessment, cleaning, deodorisation, and storage of personal property and household contents that have been affected by water, fire, or mould damage. In Australia, contents restoration is typically handled under the contents section of a home and contents policy, separate from the building restoration scope.

Pack-out refers to the physical removal of contents from the damaged property for off-site cleaning and storage while building restoration is completed. Pack-out is commonly required when:
- The building restoration will take an extended period (>2 weeks)
- Contents require specialist cleaning not achievable on-site (ultrasonics, freeze-drying for documents)
- The presence of contents would impede building drying or remediation

## The Contents Inventory

A comprehensive contents inventory is the most important document produced during a pack-out. Each item must be individually listed with:

- Item description (category, brand, model where known)
- Quantity
- Pre-loss condition (new, good, fair, poor)
- Damage description (wet, smoke-affected, broken, etc.)
- Proposed action (clean and return, replace, total loss)
- Estimated current replacement value (for total loss items)
- Photo reference number

The inventory should be completed in the presence of the insured (or their representative) where possible, and signed by both the restorer and the insured. Disputes about missing or damaged items in storage are far less common when both parties have signed an accurate inventory before items leave the site.

## Contents Cleaning Methods

Different contamination types require different cleaning approaches:

**Water-damaged contents (Category 1/2):**
- Hard surfaces: wipe-down with appropriate cleaning agents, dry thoroughly
- Soft furnishings: assessed for viability of cleaning vs replacement; Category 2 contact requires antimicrobial treatment
- Documents and photographs: freeze-drying is the most effective method for water-damaged documents; specialist services available in most Australian capital cities
- Electronics: do not power on until inspected by an electronics restorer; salt and mineral deposits from water damage can cause corrosion and short circuits

**Fire/smoke-damaged contents:**
- Ultrasonic cleaning tanks are highly effective for hard-surface items (tools, kitchenware, decorative items) with smoke residue
- Soft furnishings with smoke odour: ozone treatment in sealed chambers at the cleaning facility (safe for unoccupied items, not for occupied spaces)
- Clothing and textiles: specialist dry-cleaning processes for smoke odour removal

**Mould-affected contents:**
- Non-porous items can typically be HEPA-vacuumed, wiped, and treated with EPA-registered antimicrobial
- Porous items (upholstered furniture, mattresses, books) with active mould growth are generally unrestorable

## Storage Requirements

Contents storage must protect items from further damage:
- Climate-controlled storage for sensitive items (electronics, artwork, musical instruments, documents)
- Adequate packaging — items should be wrapped or boxed to prevent impact damage during transport and storage
- Segregation of smoke-affected from non-smoke-affected items to prevent cross-contamination
- A documented chain of custody from site to storage facility

## Insurance Documentation for Contents Claims

The insurer requires:
- Signed contents inventory with pre-loss condition and proposed action for each item
- Photos of all damaged items in situ before pack-out
- Invoices or receipts for replacement items (total loss) where available
- Storage invoices for the duration of storage
- Final return inventory confirming what was returned to the insured
videoScript:
G'day, welcome to RestoreAssist. Today we're covering contents restoration and pack-out — the process of removing, cleaning, storing, and returning personal property from a damaged home.

Contents restoration is often undervalued in the industry, but it's one of the most relationship-sensitive parts of a job. The insured's personal belongings are involved. Getting the documentation and handling right protects you and demonstrates care for the client.

The inventory is everything. Before anything leaves the site, every item needs to be individually listed — description, condition, damage, and what you propose to do with it. Clean and return, or total loss replacement. Take a photo of each item. Do this in front of the insured where possible, and get them to sign the inventory. That signature eliminates most disputes about items 'going missing' during pack-out.

For water-damaged contents — Category 1 or 2 — hard surfaces get wiped down with appropriate cleaning agents and dried thoroughly. Soft furnishings with Category 2 contact need antimicrobial treatment as well as drying. For water-damaged documents and photos, freeze-drying is the most effective method — there are specialist services for this in most Australian capital cities. Electronics: don't power them on until an electronics restorer has assessed them. Water leaves mineral deposits that cause corrosion and shorts when power is applied.

For smoke-damaged contents, ultrasonic cleaning tanks are excellent for hard-surface items — it's remarkable what ultrasonics can do with heavily soot-covered items. Clothing and textiles need specialist dry-cleaning for smoke odour. Ozone treatment in a sealed chamber at the cleaning facility is very effective for soft furnishings — note that ozone cannot be used in occupied spaces.

Storage needs to be climate-controlled for sensitive items. Electronics, artwork, musical instruments, and documents all need stable temperature and humidity. And segregate smoke-affected items from non-smoke-affected items — cross-contamination of odour is a real risk.

For the insurance claim, you need the signed inventory, in-situ photos, receipts for replacement items where available, and storage invoices. The final return inventory confirming what went back to the insured closes the loop.

RestoreAssist includes a contents inventory module in the inspection workflow so all of this is captured in one place.

Head to restoreassist.app.
